elements.sent-2411-001: Recent work on supervised learning \cite{GopalanKRSW22 defined the notion of omnipredictors, i.e., predictor functions $p$ over features that are simultaneously competitive for minimizing a family of loss functions $\calL$ against a comparator class $\calC$.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Omniprediction requires approximating the Bayes-optimal predictor beyond the loss minimization paradigm, and has generated significant interest in the learning theory community.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, even for basic settings such as agnostically learning single-index models (SIMs), existing omnipredictor constructions require impractically-large sample complexities and runtimes, and output complex, highly-improper hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our main contribution is a new, simple construction of omnipredictors for SIMs.
elements.sent-2411-005: We give a learner outputting an omnipredictor that is $\eps$-competitive on any matching loss induced by a monotone, Lipschitz link function, when the comparator class is bounded linear predictors.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our algorithm requires $\approx \eps^{-4}$ samples and runs in nearly-linear time, and its sample complexity improves to $\approx \eps^{-2}$ if link functions are bi-Lipschitz.
elements.sent-2411-007: This significantly improves upon the only prior known construction, due to \cite{mc, loss-oi , which used $\gtrsim \eps^{-10}$ samples.}
elements.sent-2411-008: We achieve our construction via a new, sharp analysis of the classical Isotron algorithm \cite{KalaiS09, KakadeKKS11 in the challenging agnostic learning setting, of potential independent interest.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Previously, Isotron was known to properly learn SIMs in the realizable setting, as well as constant-factor competitive hypotheses under the squared loss \cite{ZarifisWDD24 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: As they are based on Isotron, our omnipredictors are multi-index models with $\approx \eps^{-2}$ prediction heads, bringing us closer to the tantalizing goal of proper omniprediction for general loss families and comparators.
elements.sent-2411-011: This problem is parameterized by a feature-label distribution $\calP$ supported on $\xset \times \{0, 1\}$,\footnote{Throughout this paper, $\xset$ denotes a domain where example features lie; in our applications, $\xset \subseteq \R^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Here, we let each model $c \in \calC$ output predictions in a domain $D$, so $\ell$ evaluates the ``fit'' of the prediction $c(\vx) \in D$ against a label $y \in \{0, 1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-013: The goal in supervised learning via loss minimization is to learn a predictor $p: \xset \to D$ from samples $\sim \calP$, such that \begin{equation}\E_{(\vx, y) \sim \calP}\Brack{\ell(p(\vx), y)} \le \min_{c \in \calC} \E_{(\vx, y) \sim \calP}\Brack{\ell(c(\vx), y)} + \eps,\end{equation} where $\eps$ is an (ideally small) error parameter.
elements.sent-2411-014: Such a guarantee implies that our predictor has fit the data at least as well as the best model in $\calC$, as evaluated by $\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-015: If the predictor $p$ itself belongs to the comparator class $\calC$, we say it is proper; otherwise, we say it is improper.
elements.sent-2411-016: Recent developments in learning theory have led to a new, more stringent notion of supervised learning, known as omniprediction \cite{GopalanKRSW22 (Definition~\ref{def:omni ), which requires $p$ to be competitive, in an appropriate sense, against a family of loss functions $\calL$, rather than only a single $\ell \in \calL$.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This definition is motivated by settings where we wish to evaluate predictors using multiple loss functions of interest.
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, this could occur if losses depend on external parameters (e.g., the market price of items on a given future day) that are unknown at the time of learning.
elements.sent-2411-019: Moreover, omniprediction provides a more comprehensive view on supervised learning than (single) loss minimization.
elements.sent-2411-020: Indeed, \cite{loss-oi showed that a natural dual perspective yields a way to establish omniprediction, by showing that $p$ is indistinguishable from the Bayes-optimal predictor $p^\star(\vx) \defeq \E[y \mid \vx]$, when audited by a family of distinguishing statistical tests, parameterized by $(\ell, c) \in \calL \times \calC$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Omniprediction is a strong requirement with appealing downstream implications, and has received a flurry of follow-up interest \cite{loss-oi, omni-constrained, omni-swap, high-dim, performative, GargJRR24, GopalanORSS24 since its proposal as a goal in supervised learning.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Most of these works have focused on extending existing omnipredictor constructions to more challenging learning tasks, or characterizing the relationship between omniprediction and other notions of learning such as multicalibration \cite{mc , an influential notion of multigroup fairness with strong theoretical guarantees.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Where the theory of omniprediction has comparatively lagged behind is in its end-to-end efficient algorithmic implementation.

elements.sent-2411-025: Unfortunately, for many natural supervised learning tasks, multicalibration is known to be as hard as agnostic learning.
elements.sent-2411-026: Even for simple model classes $\calC$, e.g., halfspaces, polynomial-time weak agnostic learning is unachievable under standard complexity-theoretic assumptions \cite{GR:06, FeldmanGKP06, Daniely16 , motivating the consideration of more tractable, concrete model families.}
elements.sent-2411-027: A promising step was taken by \cite{loss-oi , who proposed to study the family of single-index models (SIM) as a basic tractable model class for omniprediction.}
elements.sent-2411-028: A SIM is a pervasive ML model, which posits that labels $y \mid \vx$ follow a relationship parameterized by the composition of a monotone link function $\sigma$, e.g., logit or ReLU, and a linear classifier $\vw$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:hope_realizable}\E\Brack{y \mid \vx} \approx \sigma\Par{\vw \cdot \vx}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-599: In this section, we give an implementation of $\BPM$ based on a well-known data structure framework called a segment tree.
elements.sent-2411-600: We state a guarantee on segment trees adapted from Lemma 8, \cite{HuJTY24 to include insertion and deletion, deferring a proof to Appendix~\ref{ssec:segtree .}}
elements.sent-2411-601: Let $v$ be an array whose size $s$ is guaranteed to be at most $n$, where each element of $v$ is initialized to be the identity element $e$ of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-602: There is a data structure $\mathcal{D}$, called a segment tree, that can perform each of the following operations in $O(\log(n))$ time (assuming semigroup products can be computed in constant time).
elements.sent-2411-603: The time counter $\tau$ is initialized to $0$, and tracks the number of times $\Update$ is called, minus the number of times $\InvUpdate$ is called.
elements.sent-2411-604: Throughout let $s \defeq |S|$, the size of the maintained set.
elements.sent-2411-605: We define our tree as follows: each leaf node $j \in [s]$ implicitly stores an ordered pair $v[j] = (k_j, h_j) \in \Z_{\ge 0 \times \R$.}
elements.sent-2411-606: We maintain the invariant that the pair $(\alpha_j, \beta_j)$ stored at each leaf by $\BPM$ satisfies \begin{equation}\label{eq:ab_from_hk}\begin{aligned}\alpha_j = \frac 1 {2\tau + 2 - 2k_j},\; \beta_j = \frac{h_j}{\tau + 1 - k_j} \text{ if } \alpha_j \neq 0, k_j = 0,\; \beta_j = h_j \text{ if } \alpha_j = 0. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Moreover, for all $j \in [s]$, $k_j$ will be fixed to the global time $\tau$ when the $j^{\text{th }$ node was inserted, i.e., it never changes throughout the node's lifetime.}
elements.sent-2411-607: We now discuss how to maintain $(h_j, k_j)$ preserving \eqref{eq:ab_from_hk via semigroup operations, using Lemma~\ref{lem:SegmentTree .}}
elements.sent-2411-608: The semigroup of interest consists of elements $\add_{t, s, p, \ell}$, parameterized by a tuple $(t, s, p, \ell) \in \Z_{\ge 0 \times \R^3$ operating on pairs in $\Z_{\ge 0 \times \R$.}}
elements.sent-2411-609: The identity element of the semigroup is $\add_{0,0,0,0}$.
elements.sent-2411-610: We enforce that if $t = 0$, then $(s, p, \ell) = (0, 0, 0)$, and for $t > 0$, the other parameters can arbitrarily vary.
elements.sent-2411-611: Intuitively, the parameters $(t, s, p)$ modifies the first coordinate of a tuple $(k_j, h_j)$, used to recover the quadratic coefficient at a leaf $\alpha_j$ using \eqref{eq:ab_from_hk .}
elements.sent-2411-612: Among these, $t$ represents the global time when an operation occurs, $s$ represents a deferred update, and $p$ represents a second-order deferred update.
elements.sent-2411-613: Similarly, the parameter $\ell$ modifies the second coordinate $h_j$ which can be used to recover the linear coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-614: The function $\add_{t, s, p,\ell}$, operating on $(k, h) \in \Z_{\ge 0 \times \R$, is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:add_forward}\add_{t,s,p,\ell}((k, h)) = \begin{cases} (k, (t+1-k)s+p+h), \text{if $k > 1$,} (k, \ell + h), \text{if $k = 0$.} \end{cases}\end{equation} It is straightforward to check that $\add_{0, 0, 0, 0}$ is an identity element.}
elements.sent-2411-615: We now define how to compose semigroup elements.
elements.sent-2411-616: Our semigroup is abelian, and we split composition into two cases.
elements.sent-2411-617: If we want to compose $\add_{t', s', p', \ell'}$ and $\add_{t, s, p, \ell}$ where $t = \min(t, t') = 0$, it must be the case that $s = p = \ell = 0$ by definition.
elements.sent-2411-618: Then in this case, we define \begin{equation}\add_{t', s', p', \ell'} \cdot \add_{t, s, p, \ell} = \add_{t, s, p, \ell} \cdot \add_{t', s', p', \ell'} = \add_{t', s', p', \ell'}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-127: \begin{equation}\add_{t', s', p', \ell'} \cdot \add_{t, s, p, \ell} = \add_{t, s, p, \ell} \cdot \add_{t', s', p', \ell'} = \add_{t', s', p', \ell'}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-619: In the other case where $t = \min(t, t') > 0$, we define \begin{equation}\label{eq:semigroup_compose}\add_{t',s',p',\ell'}\cdot\add_{t,s,p,\ell}=\add_{t,s,p,\ell}\cdot\add_{t',s',p',\ell'}= \add_{t, s+s', p+p'+(t'-t)s',\ell+\ell'},\end{equation} which is consistent with \eqref{eq:add_forward since $(t+1-k)s+(t'+1-k)s'= (t+1-k)(s+s')+ (t-t')s'$.}

elements.section-2411-006: Omnipredictors in One Dimension from PAV
elements.sent-2411-638: In this section, we focus on a basic one-dimensional setting where the domain $\xset$ consists of scalars in $\R$.
elements.sent-2411-639: When the family $\calC$ consists of non-decreasing functions (not necessarily linear), we show that running the standard PAV algorithm (see \Cref{ssec:pav ) directly gives an omnipredictor for all matching losses.}
elements.sent-2411-640: This implies the existence of an omnipredictor with a very simple structure (a non-decreasing step function) as well as a very efficient standard algorithm for learning such an omnipredictor (PAV can be implemented in linear time \cite{pav-project ).}
elements.sent-2411-641: We note that this result is equivalent to a previous result of \cite{pav-proper (\Cref{thm:pav-proper ) showing that the PAV solution simultaneously minimizes every proper loss among the family of non-decreasing predictors.}}
elements.sent-2411-642: We present a new and arguably simpler proof of both results via the notion of omnigap.
elements.sent-2411-643: We start from the easier case where the domain $\xset$ is a finite subset of $\R$, and the probability mass function of the distribution $\calP$ over $\xset\times \{0,1\}$ is fully given as input to the PAV algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-644: We will later consider general distributions over $\R\times \{0,1\}$ by treating the current $\calP$ as the empirical distribution over samples drawn i.i.d.\ from the general distribution.
elements.sent-2411-645: Let $\calP$ be an arbitrary distribution over $\xset\times \{0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-646: Then the solution $p$ from running PAV on $\calP$ is an omnipredictor w.r.t.\ the class of non-decreasing functions and all matching losses.
elements.sent-2411-647: That is, for any non-decreasing $\sigma:\R\to [0,1]$ and any non-decreasing $c:\xset \to \R$, \begin{equation}\E_{(x,y)\sim\calP}[\pl\sigma(p(x),y)] - \E_{(\vx,y)\sim\calP}[\ml\sigma(c(x),y)]\le 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-648: By \Cref{lm:omnigap-2 , \Cref{thm:1d follows immediately from the following main helper claim showing that the PAV solution has a non-positive omnigap w.r.t.\ any non-decreasing function.}}
elements.sent-2411-649: We prove \Cref{prop:1d-omnigap using the following lemma, where all expectations are over $(x,y)\sim \calP$:.}
elements.sent-2411-650: The PAV solution $p$ is perfectly calibrated: $\E[y|p(x) = v] = v$ for every $v$ in the range of $p$.
elements.sent-2411-651: Moreover, for any non-decreasing function $\xi:\xset\to \R$, it holds that \begin{equation}\label{eq:1d-lemma-goal} \E[(p(x) - y)\xi(x)] \ge 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-652: We recall the construction of the output predictor $p$ in the PAV algorithm (see \Cref{ssec:pav ).}
elements.sent-2411-653: For every $x$ in a block $B$ in the final partition $\calB$, the value of $p(x)$ is defined to be $p^\star_B := \E[y|x\in B]$.
elements.sent-2411-654: It thus suffices to prove the lemma per block.
elements.sent-2411-655: Conditioned on each block, the function $p(x)$ is a constant function with value $p^\star_B$, so the calibration guarantee of $p$ follows from $\E[y|x\in B] = p^\star_B$.
elements.sent-2411-656: To prove \eqref{eq:1d-lemma-goal , it suffices to prove \begin{equation}\label{eq:1d-lemma-goal-2} \E[(p^\star_B - y)\xi(x)|x\in B]\ge 0, \quad \text{for every $B\in \calB$},\end{equation} where $\calB$ is the partition after the final iteration of the PAV algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-657: We will prove a stronger result that \eqref{eq:1d-lemma-goal-2 holds for the partition $\calB$ after the $t^{\text{th }$ iteration of PAV for every $t \ge 0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-658: We first show that the following prefix expectation is always non-negative: \begin{equation}\label{eq:1d-8} \E_{x\in B, x \le u}[y - p^\star_B] \ge 0, \quad \text{for every $B\in \calB$ and $u\in B$}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-136: \begin{equation}\label{eq:1d-8} \E_{x\in B, x \le u}[y - p^\star_B] \ge 0, \quad for every $B\in \calB$ and $u\in B$.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-659: Here the expectation is over $(x,y)\sim \calP$ conditioned on $x\in B$ and $x\le u$.
elements.sent-2411-660: We prove this by induction on $t$.
